segments.text: Where's our green scope? We don't know who.
Don't worry. They'll be here.
Doesn't matter. Okay. All right.
Nice to see you, and thank you for coming.
Now, we've got five minutes with you.
You're our first submitter. You've done this before.
Once.
Once. And for anyone listening,
everyone has read the papers, so you don't need to laboriously go
through the papers. What would be helpful, especially with only five minutes, leave
a
little bit of time for one question also.
Okay.
All right. Fantastic. I'm just trying to give you a bit of advice, but more
generally to anyone submitting this morning.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Are you ready?
You're... Please proceed, John.
Ah, thank you.
Thanks for giving me this opportunity to speak to my submission.
When I think about the increasingly punitive
incarceration policies of this coalition, more people
locked up, disenfranchisement of all prisoners, and
now this amendment that will allow, it seems to
me, longer periods of solitary confinement,
I ask myself five basic questions.
Why is New Zealand's incarceration rate so much higher than
Australia's or England's, double Canada's,
and double Ireland's?
And why is the government planning and budgeting to increase
incarceration still further by more than a third,
and women by two thirds?
Why is our recidivism rate so high?
60% within two years of release. Obviously, we're
managing rehabilitation and reintegration very
badly.
Will more incarceration and allowing prisoners to be kept
in solitary for 13 days
make for a safer society?
I think not, or we wouldn't have to keep locking up more and more people.
Common sense tells us that to keep doing the same thing over and over and
expecting a different result is unsound, to put it
kindly.
Are the billions of dollars being spent locking up prisoners the way we do
an efficient use of our tax dollars?
Again, obviously not. If it were, our
incarceration and recidivism rates would be going down, and
they're not.
And do punitive measures such as solitary confinement and
disenfranchisement help towards rehabilitation and reintegration?
That one's rhetorical.
For several years, up until COVID, I was a volunteer in
Rimutaka Prison. I worked with prisoners across all security
levels.
In the high security pods, I saw it firsthand the effect of
lockdowns and short staffing problems, when prisoners might get
an hour out of their cells a day, and it was not good.
At such times, they'd come to literacy lessons for an hour
out of their cell, even if it was schoolwork, but in no state
to learn.
No one could say this was conducive to rehabilitation.
In contrast, where effective things were happening, such as the few
trade training courses, the difference was stark.
These students were proud of their achievements.
They had hopes of a job on release. More than one said to me,
"This is my last lag."
New Zealand has ratified the Convention Against Torture and Other Cruel,
Inhuman or Degrading Treatment or Punishment.
We've designated national preventative measures, including the
Office of the Ombudsman and the Human Rights Commission, to make
recommendations to government on these issues, and they do.
In
his report after visiting a maximum security peru unit
18 months ago,
then Ombudsman Peter Boshier stated unequivocally,
"I saw compelling evidence of prolonged solitary confinement in
oppressive conditions of all those in custody in the peru,
as well as other human rights abuses, including concerning
incidents of the use of force. I consider the cumulative
human impact
of the conditions and treatment of the people in custody in the peru
to be cruel, inhuman and degrading." The
Ombudsman's own words.
And now the coalition wants to extend incarceration and
solitary confinement provisions.
Locking up prisoners in solitary may be
an easy option for prisoner management in the short term,
but many studies have shown the serious psychoses that result
from it. If prolonged, these psychoses become
irreversible, hard-baked into prisoners who will eventually be
released into society. It's not rehabilitation,
and it certainly isn't preparation for reintegration.
I'm asking the select committee to please think seriously about
the effect this amendment will have,
and to advise the government to withdraw it in its current form. Thank you.
Okay. We're just about out of time, but we'll have one question of anyone.
Right. Dr. Tracey McLennan.
Thank you. I just wanted to commend the work that you've
done.
Oh, thank you.
It's not easy, and it's not everybody's
first priority, and it makes a huge-
... difference, so I want to acknowledge that.
You mentioned training, and I just wanted to know, can you give me an
example of the types of positive kind of-
Well, the one I saw that I don't think it's happening anymore, they
built a house from scratch, from the ground up.
And this was in the lower security area inside the wire.
And the people working on it did
Everything, from the pouring of-
yeah, everything, including painting and paper hanging.
And one of them that I was doing NCEA
with said to me,
"I was working out there the other day, and a group from the industry
came in. One of them tapped me on the shoulder and said, 'I have a job for you
when you come out.'"
Mm.
And that was so positive.
Thank you, Mr. Bonnett, for turning up and particularly in person.
It's always much more compelling.
Thank you very much.
Cool. Right. Now, Max Marshall.
He's on Zoom, Max. He's on Zoom. I thought he was going to be here in person.
Oh.
Mr. Marshall, if you're there, we're ready for you.
All right.
We were looking forward to seeing you this morning, but obviously other
plans took hold.
Yeah. That's all right. Sorry about-
No, that's all right.
I-
Hey, we've got five minutes with you, Max.
Okay. Good stuff.
Good morning, everyone. Thank you for giving me the opportunity
to
speak.
What I
want to do is
talk to you
about the
concept of dangerousness with respect to one particular
subcategory inside the profile of
dangerous sexual offenders, that of the minor
attracted individual, whose interest has a
biological dimension.
For some
minor attracted individuals experienced in law, the
guarantees of natural justice have not prevailed.
I am interested in what shapes the concern for this group in prison.
Much controversy arose that occurs around
decisions for future safety of public.
And again, I want to stress the randomness of law cannot reconcile
risk and capable risk. Some
historical context. The gay liberation movement
fight was framed around sexual consent.
Prior to law reform, public toilets were used as meeting
places where men had sex with other men, and among these encounters
were many with young people. The police's
attempt to block centered around the use of this public space
other than the use intended. Interesting to note,
assumptions were that the young person would not be having sex with
the older person. Rather, the police
saw it only as part of the gay lifestyle career.
The move to remove many public toilets came at the same
time as law reform. And while it gave us the appearance of becoming
a more liberal society, at the same time, the boundary
between sex between an adult and a young person today
has become a further intense boundary, one
which must not be crossed. And while we agree with the sentiment
born this way should mean precisely that, the profile of
a minor attracted person is too hard for many.
Even more difficult is the idea of a young person having
agency in these situations.
The therapy for those incarcerated follows two
forms.
The first one attempts to get the person to see their sexuality
as erroneous. The second attempts to
conform his or her sexuality
to
appear more normal to society.
I argue that this is bad therapy.
Both stigmatize rather than provide help.
What has not been explored is how the experience of the
inmate as an individual critiques and
challenges assumptions about what the content of what
normal involves.
This insistence that of just deserves sentencing is part of an extended
process, which begins when the police and prosecution decide
on how to process suspects. The application of
the term dangerous for this profile is unfair, if
not prejudicial.
Where punishments may appear to statutes of law, many key decisions are
not visible. The challenge presented by this group pose
no easy answers,
but failure is guaranteed for the process of developing solutions if we are
automatically going to view this group as dangerous.
But this is what shapes many lawmakers and society at large.
All right. Hey, thank you, Max.
Very nice to see you again, and thank you again for your contribution. All right?
Right. We are moving on to the New Zealand Law
Society, which are via Zoom. Hey, good morning, Kerry. How are you going?
Are you on your own? You got Matt with you? Oh, here you are. Cool.
Right then, we've got 10 minutes with you.
Good morning. Thank you, sir. Yes, so,
I'm Matt McKillop from the Human Rights and Privacy Committee of the Law
Society, and with Mr. Cook from the Criminal Law
Committee.
Thank you for the opportunity to speak to our submission in this matter.
I'll start by saying that there's a lot of good in this bill,
and I don't want to-- just because we're going to be focusing
on a
big issue, I don't want to skip that.
We support the bill's intention to establish a clear statutory
basis for the safe, humane, and lawful management of this small group of
very high-risk prisoners.
We do, however, have serious concerns as to some aspects of the
proposals , principally
That if adopted, the amendments would sanction treatment that falls
below international human rights norms.
I won't be repeating our written submission,
but will simply start by drawing the committee's attention to those
main human rights issues that we would
suggest require
change. And
I'll then hand over to Mr. Cook, who'll speak about some other
proposed drafting
improvements, which will focus on just one or two of the most
essential ones in this oral submission.
So, to start with those human rights
concerns, the Law Society supports the creation of this
statutory process for designated management
prisoners.
The present lack of any statutory process
for identifying people in this group has been a subject of
criticism by the Chief Ombudsman, the Chief Inspector of
Corrections.
However, as our submission outlines, the
provision in the bill for minimum
meaningful human contact
has some problems. This, I'm talking about the rule
that would be set out in new Section 69A,
and that's opposed by the Law Society in its present form.
The reasons for that are as follows.
At international law, the concepts of solitary
confinement, prolonged solitary
confinement, and meaningful human contact emerged from
the
Mandela Rules, as they're called
for short. Those rules
are an instrument adopted by the UN General
Assembly
as a
group of universal norms relating to how prisons ought to
be run.
But it's not just a UN
aspiration, it's also expressly integrated into our
act in Section Five of
the Principal Act as one of the purposes of our
regime.
The bill, as drafted, would not be achieving
Mandela rules consistency,
and that's really the point that we make at paragraph
4.17 of our submission, that the
10-hour
meaningful human contact in any 14-day period
would be less than half of what would be expected in order
to meet those
standards.
And that
base level in the Mandela Rules is around avoiding
harm to detainees.
We've also noted that we think Cabinet initially made a call that
it should be
the Mandela Rules level, and that
seems to have changed without too much
reconsideration. And I just wanted to note that while
Peru Unit was created to house a particular
serious offender,
he's not representative of these prisoners as a
whole. These are often serious offenders who have had very
traumatic early lives,
involving
their own physical or sexual
abuse, gang
upbringings,
often also have mental health or intellectual disability or other sorts
of issues that
contribute to their risk. And
socializing these guys at a level that falls below the minimum level in
the Mandela Rules risks further harm to these
prisoners.
So, I'm
rushing through here because of the time. I'll hand over to Mr.
Cook now to
pick that up.
Morning. The Law Society believes that
our submissions have some essential matters that really could sharpen the drafting
of the designation test and strengthen other aspects of the proposed new
regime, and these include the addition of "imminent" to the
proposed test of extreme risk, i.e., an imminent extreme risk
would be preferable. Our submission discusses this at 3.8 and
The Law Society further suggests that the designation test could require the chief
executive to determine that the imminent extreme risk to prison or public
safety cannot be mitigated by ordinary
management measures reasonably available to corrections, and can
only be properly addressed by designation and restrictive management.
Restrictive management, with all that it entails, should only be considered if
the risks of that particular prisoner cannot be sufficiently mitigated by less
intrusive and rights infringing conduct.
We also recommend that the test for designation in sections 52
[C] and 52 is too broad, should be revised to
solely respond to extreme risk that is present whilst a person is
detained in the prison. There will be prisoners who pose an extreme
risk of serious offending if released from prison, but who do not pose any
risk when managed in custody subject to the usual regime applying in that prison
unit. And that group is not the bill's concern and do not
warrant the restrictions or resources.
Also, the new section 52K sub one in clause
12 of the bill creates a duty for the department to
inform the chief executive if the department becomes aware of information
sufficient to justifying a review of the designation.
Whilst it's relatively common to define the functions of public service agencies
in statute, it seems unusual to confer a duty on a
department rather than a natural or legal person.
Reformulating the duty as one held by the chief executive would be
preferable, and we've also raised the question of designation of persons
under 25. If provision to do so is retained,
should it be a different test? The committee may wish to consider this aspect.
And our submission raises other points relating to segregation, prisoners' trust
accounts, and legal representation. But Mr.
McKillop or myself are happy to answer any questions should you have any in the
very short time you have available.
Good. I've got one from Mr. Dan Rosewarne.
Thanks, Chair. I just want to turn to your comment around roles of
the panel and the chief executive.
In paragraph 3.19 of your submission, you raise a situation where
the chief executive departs from the panel's recommendation.
I just want to get a sense of-
... how real that litigation risk is.
And can you think of a scenario where the CE would not
follow the panel's recommendation?
I'm happy to answer this.
So the reason that we have included that is because
it does put the independence of the decision-maker
as the CE, he is independent, and there may be
situations in which he takes a different view, listening to his staff rather than
the panel.
That is problematic because it does create a litigation risk, and
given that this is in a situation where the rights are going to be zealously
guarded by individuals that have very little rights, we think that litigation risk
is real.
Why did you suggest putting the word imminent?
If someone's an extreme risk
and clearly must have done
something to be deemed to be an extreme risk, why do you have to put a
present tense of imminent in front of it?
I cannot understand why you're recommending that.
So a risk that's distant or speculative may never materialize
or may be overtaken by intervening events.
The closer a risk is to occurring, the more reliably you can assess whether it will
actually happen.
The more imminent a risk, the less time there is to monitor, reassess,
or apply lesser interventions. Imminence therefore justifies acting now
rather than waiting for more information. There's also proportionality issues.
A remote risk may not warrant intervention.
The cost or burden of the response would outweigh the benefit.
Imminence tests the balance-
But if a prisoner has already exhibited
actions that are reprehensible,
and I'm not just talking about a certain individual, but
have done that, and they've clearly been classified because of what they've done.
Why would you then say they have to be imminent?
Because if a person has got to that stage, they've
been deemed, because there's very few people deemed that way.
Well, imminence also requires discipline in terms of the analysis.
It prevents a remote or theoretical risk from being used to justify
disproportionate action today, and that's particularly important in human rights
law. Ultimately,
if that person poses a risk, then the imminence of that
risk is going to be an important factor in determining whether or not you want to
impose these very-
Can I rephrase the question?
... significant restrictions.
Mr. Cook, I wonder if I can ask that a different way, because imminence
in its kind of natural language
makes it sound like
they're on the cusp of violence
at every moment.
When you say imminent, do you simply mean that
the risk of serious violence or an incident
is real and immediate and
ongoing rather than historic?
Yes. So they're not being judged merely upon maybe the actions that
resulted in the imprisonment, but it's an ongoing risk that has some
immediacy about it.
Okay. All right, cool. Hey, thank you to both of you for joining us this
morning.
Thank you for the invitation.
Very helpful.
Yeah.
All right, cool.
Thank you.
All right, thank you.
Thanks very much.
See you both.
All right. I think we've got Amanda Hill. Right.
I saw you come in, so please come over here.
We've got 10 minutes with you.
Kia ora koutou.
My name is Amanda Hill. With me is Farroz Broushman-
Farroz
... who's a lawyer in my firm.
Thank you for your time today.
My practice focuses on public law cases, which often involve people who
are detained.
And I do that work because I have
an underlying belief that prisons can be lawfully managed in a way that
promotes rehabilitation and also meets our desire for punishment, which is a
really natural human sentiment. And those two things don't have to be
mutually exclusive. So that's how I wanted to start out.
There are two buckets, rehabilitation and lawful treatment, and
punishment, and they don't take away from each other.
I was here to listen to the Law Society submissions, and I'd like to
endorse
the things I heard there.
And I'm not going to repeat those, as Mr.
McKillop stole my point about Section 5 of the Corrections
Act, bringing the Mandela Rules into
our legislation, and I'm not going to rehash that ground, and I'm not going to go
over our written submissions. I just think it is
important that when we draft legislation, it is in accordance with the things that
we've signed up to at United Nations level, and that goes to our baseline
reputation as an international actor and someone
who sticks with what we sign up for, in
short.
My starting point is that this bill shouldn't progress.
That it
seeks to implement what is already existing in the PRU, which is found to be
unlawful, rather than try to elevate the PRU to
something that meets our legal obligations.
However, I'm also a realist. I recognize that this bill may pass if
it goes through the whole process within this parliamentary
session. And reflecting on that, we'll also try and suggest some
amendments
to the bill that might try and improve it.
One of the key things, and I think the Law Society dealt with this, is the
issue of meaningful human contact.
How much should a person have, and what is the quality of that?
The bill currently provides for around a third of the international
obligation of two hours of meaningful human contact a day.
So it's a third of what we've signed up to at the UN.
It also decreases the quality of that contact.
Best practice and expert opinion is that meaningful human contact is in person like
we are here now, not with barriers, not over the
phone, that we become better people when we're in a room with each other.
My clients are in the PRU. I have eight clients who are either in the
PRU or have
been in the PRU. They forget how to talk. They forget how to converse.
They forget how to read body language.
And when you are talking about risk of violence, an inability
to read body language, an inability to talk,
those go to creating risk.
So I'd like to just remind you of what the importance of being in a room.
And so we say that if you are going to have this
type of unit, then the legislation must match the two hours
requirement in the Mandela Rules.
Anything less sets a floor that is less than our
obligations.
And it has to ensure that there is good quality, meaningful contact.
And a lot of the other provisions might limit that.
So,
cell adjacent yards remove meaningful human contact.
So some of those provisions
put a dent in the ways in which people can
be together. And
I think that it's important to make sure that the legislation reflects those
things.
We want to talk a bit about what we call the entry and exit to the
PRU, because I think there is some amendments that would be really
helpful there to ensure that there are robust processes.
But one thing I want to touch on before I hand over to Mr.
Brailsford, because I want to leave some time for questions.
I'm one of the few people that have been in the PRU, and I can talk a lot about it.
I think I'm able to answer some questions that you might have.
But one of my baseline requirements, and one of the things I think is really
important, that this bill should provide that any person who
has a diagnosed mental illness or an intellectual disability should
not be put in the prisons of extreme risk unit.
It is very clear, and our own Supreme Court has found that if you place
someone with known mental health vulnerabilities or intellectual
disabilities in the PRU,
that meets the definition of torture when they are under prolonged solitary
confinement. There's no leeway there.
If you take someone with those disabilities, you are immediately in
breach. So I just want to be really clear, and that goes to what assessments and
what evidence we have
or what the decision maker has before them when they are looking at should someone
go into this regime.
I will pause there and have Farros talk about
the changes we'd suggest to entry and exit to the PRU.
All right, cool. I've got a couple of questions.
Certainly.
Yeah.
So the first thing is
shifting it from the panel and the chief executive to the High
Court.
Adopting the approach that we have around public protection
orders and ESOs, those are the types of mechanisms we're using to
manage extreme or ongoing imminent risk.
And it's appropriate for those decisions to be made
in a really independent way, and the High Court offers that and offers some
rigor
to that decision making.
Again, I won't take you to those provisions, but I think
that's to be strongly preferred
over, as the Law Society recognized, a panel
advising a statutory officer, and it just starts to get murky
in terms of where the
clarity and submissions from the person subject to those orders might
go. The next is around notifications, and I talk about this
in one particular place in our written submissions, but it also
applies in relation to the destruction of
property.
I think the provisions could just be strengthened to make clear that the
reasons given to a prisoner enable them
to challenge or fully understand the decision being
made or the potential decision to be made.
And that's based on some of the other notification requirements that we see in the
Corrections Act, where there are reasons given, but
really only in summary and not sufficient for someone to fully understand
how that decision's been reached.
And given the significance of these types of orders,
it's important that the person subject to them understands them fully.
Okay, cool. Right, I'm going to go to Mr-
I'm happy to leave.
Do you want to do it?
Tracy McLellan.
Dr. Tracy McLellan.
Thank you. I just wanted a little bit more information about that judicial decision
making. So is that comparable to other types of decisions that would
always be made in a judicial way as opposed to a panel?
Can you give me an example just to help me orient that?
So my understanding is that with public protection orders,
which is the Essentially detention of someone
that served their sentence in a prison or prison-like setting, those would always
be made by the High Court. In terms of an ESO, an extended supervision order,
which is extending parole-like conditions for someone that
presents a risk post-release, but not the same
level of risk as necessitating,
I guess, continued detention under a PPO.
That is made either by the district or High Court, depending on the
nature of the offense for which they were convicted.
So just for clarity, can you think of any other kind of decision that would be
made in this proposed way that's of kind of an
equivalent importance? Do we make
decisions via the CE and, for something that you would
see as having the same kind of litigation risk or the same
importance?
Decisions around placement in prisons are often considered to be
the purview of the chief executive, but they're often delegated.
Mm-hmm.
And so senior advisors or regional managers, or other
people make those decisions, and currently, decisions about the parole are made by
the commissioner. So there's no sunlight on that.
So there are... Sorry.
I would add, I think it's really general
placement in terms of the unit or prison.
Those, I don't think are really analogous to placement in the parole,
because
if you're shifting someone from a low
security unit to a high security unit because of conduct, that is
really quite operational. You're not significantly changing
the regime to which they're subject in the way that you are when you're subjecting
them to a directed restricted management.
All right. Hey, thank you very much for coming this morning and presenting.
I really appreciate that.
Could I have 30 seconds to just one more suggested amendment.
In your segregation orders, there's a proposal for a five-day effectively
slip rule. I think that should be removed.
Segregation orders should be read strictly.
And, the five-day rule, I would suggest, would be an amendment that would be
appropriate to take that part out.
All right. Thank you.
Thank you very much.
Great. Right. People Against Prisons.
I think Ruth is on the line, is that right?
Good morning. How are you?
I'm well, thanks. Morana, can you hear me all right?
We can, and we've got you for 10 minutes.
All right, great.
Tena koutou katoa. I'm Ruth, and I'm
submitting on behalf of People Against Prisons Aotearoa.
We are a prison abolitionist organization working for a safer,
fairer Aotearoa.
Thank you for having me. This is my second time submitting to select committee on
behalf of PAPA and my first time submitting to the Justice Committee.
Today, I'm going to highlight a few parts of our full submission,
the inadequate standard for meaningful human contact, and the
introduction of a very broad definition for segregating prisoners.
I missed the Law Society submission but have picked up from the following
submitters that the Mandela Rules have already been named and discussed as
something that submitters want to see for our country.
And I will touch on those, but not in great depth because it sounds like those have
been covered. We do note that some parts of the bill,
and especially its overall stated intent, is to reduce harm
to designated management prisoners.
However, the designations that are proposed really lack transparency.
And most of the bill reinforces a prison system which gives too much discretionary
power to corrections staff without independent oversight.
So those are the things that we're concerned about and call on the committee to
reject.
Hmm.
We also do understand that some of the people being kept in solitary
confinement or as designated management prisoners
are not particularly sympathetic or, as I think the previous submitter said,
have behaviors that
do require management and a different level of
approach, of operational approach.
However, we don't believe this justifies maltreatment and torture.
Those tools are not rehabilitative, and they don't
contribute to the wellbeing of the people who are kept in
prisons.
So to focus on our two specific places from our written submission,
the minimum meaningful human contact
in the bill is absolutely inadequate.
It must be strengthened to 14 hours per week in line with the international
standards set by the Mandela rules.
These global standards are adopted by the United Nations, and we call on
the committee to uphold the same standard here in this country.
As we all know, solitary confinement increases the risk of suicide
and self-harm, and it is classified as torture
internationally. The bill requires that prisoners receive only
10 hours of meaningful contact in every 14-day period, and this is
30% less than the global standard.
And while the bill names the global standard as desirable, it
doesn't propose it as a legal requirement. This is inadequate.
A non-binding aspiration does not provide meaningful protection for prisoners
against the harms of solitary confinement.
It also decreases their chance of rehabilitation back into society.
We also have the same concerns as other submitters have talked about with the
bill's definition of meaningful human contact, which includes telephone call.
The kind
of contact that really protects a person from psychological harms of
isolation requires that in-person face-to-face interaction.
The comments made by the previous submitter around critical exposure to other
humans in order to retain the ability to read body
language and emotional expressions is exactly what we're talking
about. And furthermore, the bill itself acknowledges that phone calls
are inadequate by noting that it is desirable for a prisoner to have
contact not only by telephone call.
So again, this is another place where we call on the committee to require this as a
legal requirement rather than a non-binding aspiration.
So we call on the committee to amend Section 69 of the proposed
bill to require 14 hours of meaningful face-to-face contact which
cannot be met by telephone.
The second area I wanted to draw attention to from our written proposal are the
grounds for segregation. These new grounds are dangerously broad,
and they can easily be used to punish prisoners.
So the three new grounds that the bill proposes to introduce
do not require that any offending or harm has actually occurred.
They require only that a prison manager forms the opinion that harm might occur.
This is extremely discretionary and very dangerous
in nature without the transparent oversight and the checks and
balances. It allows subjective opinions of an individual to
influence the welfare of a prisoner.
We're strongly opposed to these provisions.
The existing segregation grounds already give corrections staff significant
discretion. These new additions extend that discretion into
territory that is inherently subjective.
And we're also aware, through our support networks of those who are incarcerated,
that prisoners who do use the proper channels to formally seek their rights be met
are often segregated and/or transferred between prisons as a form
of punishment. So since we know this practice is already happening, we
see the new definitions of segregation as another step in this direction,
allowing the discretion of corrections staff to determine who is segregated
when. The previous submitter discussed the importance of a prisoner
knowing the basis on which they were segregated into DMP status.
But based on what we already know is happening, increasing this definition will
only worsen the situation of providing the insufficient information for the
people who actually are incarcerated on what decisions have been made around them.
So segregation is already happening in our prisons.
It does not need to be expanded. We call on the committee to reject the
new segregation grounds in the Section 58 (1) (c) and in their entirety.

Yeah.
All right. And-
So please do something about this.
Thank you, Jennifer, for sharing that today.
Thank you.
All right.
Right, now we're going to the Law Association, again by Zoom.
Marie, are you with us?
Hello? We can't hear you. Oh, good morning.
Thank you. Can you hear me now?
Yes, we can. How are you?
I'm fine, thank you. And I'm appearing this morning with
Isabella Devlin, a young barrister who is very involved in
human rights, parole, and
post-conviction rights as well.
So thank you for the time this morning.
I know that you have read our report, which is
quite formal and obviously legalese.
But what I would really like to do this morning,
and not really go through those
points in a formal way, is just to
talk to you about my actual experience
within the system over many years, and I regret
to say it has been 40 years now since my admission
as a defense trial lawyer, and I have
acted, given a lot on all levels of
classification, ex-prisoners, corrections staff,
and police consultative groups, and with those since
2009. And what my comment to
start with is,
one day these prisoners, most of them,
very few are locked up forever They will be your neighbors,
and what sort of neighbors do you want released back into the
community? I've heard a lot today about the focus on
rehabilitation and reintegration.
They are my focus today,
while not rejecting, of course, that deterrence and punishment
are all aspects that prisoners and offenders have
to deal with. I was involved throughout the regime
of BMR. I had a client. We know that
was ruled unlawful, even by Crown, before they
started. I've been very heavily involved with Odyssey
House right from the time of triple bunking
as an alternative to prison throughout bail and
sentencing. They were very fulfilling days,
and we got great legislation from the High
Court and Court of Appeal through cases whereby
we offered that alternative, and there were so many
people that did not come back before the court.
They dealt with drug and alcohol problems.
They dealt with offending. I've been involved with the prison
youth programs in the '80s, the terrible
glue sniffing days, where there were places for
the youth to go just to learn skills.
Huge gaps we pointed to in their offending
history while that PIPS program was in place.
So for me and my colleagues,
and those of us who are behind the submissions,
rehabilitation critically does reduce crime,
deals with recidivism, and provides members of
society with worthwhile lives with their
family. So the bottom line you will note from
our submissions is that important point,
the bill guarantees only about 43 minutes a day
of human contact. That's roughly a third of the Mandela
threshold. Who decides? A prisoner can be
designated supreme risk by a corrections manager
alone. No judge, no required
psychological assessment,
no
also sort of legal
persons that have to be on there. And as the last speaker
just spoke, rehabilitation pathways, these
prisoners are taken out of the normal classification system
that would otherwise let them work towards lowering
security, aiming for parole, more
rehabilitative settings. And that, as I say,
is that's the neighbor you will be inheriting,
because from what we have seen and what we deal
with on a personal level
is that we see, as the lawyers, those of
us who have prisoners in parole, in Peru, but I've
had prisoners throughout a number of years.
What I see at the moment, generally in prisons,
because there are no real rehabilitation programs
available, but in Peru, and I can
say, and we can say as lawyers, personality
changes, withdrawn, great reduction in
communication, clear signs of anxiety and
depression, and they express intense loneliness,
hopelessness, distress. We have
noticed increased difficulty in them
concentrating, confusion with what they're
in for, what they should be doing, memory loss,
and of course, that's the psychological long-term damage.
We're noticing weight loss, fatigue, headaches, all
sorts of things. And unfortunately, under this
legislation, New Zealand is really risking,
and is in breach of international laws and falling
well behind below the standard set by our
allies. This will risk severe
international reputational damaging
credibility on the world stage.
So in terms of what
we all see,
and from our experience,
a likely outcome will be years of litigation,
legal uncertainty for the regime, people
continuing to be held under conditions whose
lawfulness is contested the whole time,
and as I say, I've worked with the BMR,
I can say that is what is
coming.
That is coming before us, and great
miscarriages of justice are before us as well.
The
two final points I'd like to make, because
I do want, in case there's questions,
really emphasizing the importance of psychological
assessment as to risk before someone goes
into Peru and at every review
stage. And it may well be suggested that
people are in Peru because of things they have
done. I wish to emphasize from
experience, and those of my colleagues, people are being
placed in the Peru with no conviction history

why we are very strongly of the view

independent to corrections.
So I have
gone through fairly quickly, but I know I've seen how quickly the
10 minutes gets eaten up.
All right. No, that's good. Now I've got a question first from Dr.
Tracy McLennan.
Oh, sorry, Mr. Chair, you've just answered my question about the-
Ah

I just wanted to make sure that that was one of the things that you were wanting to
highlight-
Yes

Next question. You've talked about the designated
management prisoner process,
talking about independence and transparency.
So our understanding is that there's a panel that
helps the assessment of a prisoner, and then
it's ultimately the decision of the chief executive.
Is that your understanding of it?
Yes, but our point is that the final
decision really ought to be as we do with
other, and it's been mentioned earlier,
other restrictions on prisoners' release and
freedom and also what happens within the prisons is
subject to judicial consideration as the
final point. Say, for example, you might have
district court judges visiting-
Aren't you just opening it up? Every
prisoner, if they feel wronged, will want to do a judicial

So how do you mitigate against making sure
that you normalize
normal decision-making, making sure it's done in a transparent,
independent, and proper manner, as opposed to always

may be
just vexatious or without cause?
I can say that there are very few

We really are a good stopgap where if anything is
vexatious or within the rules,
most of us are pretty reasonable where we say we give them an ear, we
listen to them, and then we explain just what is
required by a judicial review or appeal or challenge.
And unless you have a basis, it's very clear you don't even
get off the ground. So if you look and see how many times
do we as lawyers take decisions, maybe on appeal or
on judicial review, they are very, very few.
It's often said, for example, everybody who's
famous or sports gets suppression.
They don't in reality. We tell them how hard it is.
So we do not see that. Once we get a system
in place whereby there is significant
and proper
opportunities for a very good
review or challenge to something, then you'll find
they won't be vexatious, they won't be multitudinal,
they won't even get legal aid unless you make out a case.
And most of us just give people
the line and say, "Look, thank you.
I know how you feel, but we haven't got the grounds." We don't have the
time or the resources to take
on spurious vexatious cases. And
the courts don't. They will reject them.
Case law will get better.
Yeah. So tell me, how many
cases have been taken to court that you're aware of?
You've obviously practiced for a long time.
Well, I'm working very closely at the moment challenging
Peru at every...
And I might as well be open about that, and
I am challenging that and going through the system.
It's a long system, and I found that out with BMR.
But we have to get the system in place
where there is justice and fairness, and then the
courts can say and set up the precedent
cases, so we have guidelines to say when
we can go and when we can't. And there really, as I
say, are very few. I have very few. I do appeals,
but I do appeals when I've got a basis.
I don't do a great many, and I certainly can't
remember the last time I did judicial review.
But I'm working very hard at challenging at this level.
Like I did with Odyssey House. I ended up
deputy chair of Odyssey House, and
we got a great deal of legislation and cases in
place whereby we, Odyssey House, and still
remains the top
rehabilitation place where there is real
alternatives to prison through bail and through
sentencing.
So-
Can be done
So just to be clear, you would be comfortable for a
process of judicial
review by the courts, not on the facts of the case,

Yes.
But you're recommending, I just want to be really clear, you're recommending
that there should be the opportunity for judicial review of
the classification of a prisoner or whatever it might be, or the
rights. Is that what you're suggesting?
Yes. And the final decision as to
the classification itself, whether somebody should be in Peru,
is made by somebody
who has judicial experience and is-
Yeah
... judicial. Yes. Thank you.
Yeah. Okay. All right. Hey, thank you, Marie and
Isabella, for joining us this morning.
Thank you.
Very good to hear
from you again, which is really appreciative.
Okay, thank you. Now we're going to the Royal Australasian-
Well, Australian and New Zealand, but we call it Australasian College of
Psychiatrists.
Dr. James,
are you with us?
He was.
Is?
Oh.
I don't-
Yeah
think that's right.
Yeah, I think that's right. Yeah. I was just trying to have a look at-
Sorry, it just seems to be dropping out there.
Hey, James.
Yeah.
You're not on Zoom. Oh, you are.
There we go.
Oh, very good. Nice to see you.
Yeah.
You've been with us before, and now we've-
... got 10 minutes with you.
No, that's great. Thank you. So just briefly, my name's Dr.
James Cavaney. I'm the chair of the
Australian New Zealand College of Psychiatry, Faculty of Forensic
Psychiatry. So we're the psychiatrists that work in the
prisons and obviously the forensic units where people come and
go.
I'm just processing Ms. Dyberg's
presentation. A lot of that
overlaps with what we were wishing to present, and then I'll
just proceed as we were. It maybe would just
consolidate some of her points.
A bit of it's beyond my
expertise,
but there is a case I might speak to that potentially might
identify some of those appeals and litigation processes
that she was referring to. So look, we are very
appreciative of this opportunity to present.
We nominally oppose the bill, but we're hoping
in a constructive way. It's what we've
identified
as a gap in the legislation, which potentially
introduces the clinical expertise that we
hold. So we're also interested
in the
designated
management prisoners
issue.
Recognizing there are some positive changes in terms of
the minimum
requirement
to be out of solitary confinement, if you like, and the human
contact aspects.
We do acknowledge within a prison environment, there is a
need for restrictive practice for some inmates.
But our primary concern is in focusing on the
behaviors of inmates, we're losing
opportunities to identify potential clinical
drivers of that behavior, and in so doing, we're
also losing opportunities to intervene,
provide treatment options, alternative
settings to avoid what Ms. Dyberg characterized, all
the sequelian consequences of being in isolation and the
adverse impacts on people's mental health.
Where our evidence base and the scientific literature shows
that this can often lead to an
exacerbation of existing mental health problems.
So that's the premise for our hopefully constructive opposition.
The groups that we have most concern about as a
forensic faculty, and these are people we work with on a daily basis and see on a
daily basis,
are people with neurodiversity, so the autistic
spectrum type of presentations, possibly
ADHD would be
linked to that, and the intellectual
disability population. And of course,
people with
diagnosable intellectual disabilities do end up in custodial
prisons. We've seen people with Down syndrome and IQs in their
50s on remand, having smashed a chocolate machine and not
being evicted from their supported accommodation.
So these people do enter the prison system
and don't respond well,
act out and
get restrained, et cetera. The second group we just want to
identify are people with trauma
histories.
The committee, I'm sure, was aware of post-traumatic stress disorder,
but a lot of these prisoners,
particularly Maori prisoners, do have quite profound
histories of trauma, sexual abuse, physical abuse, and the nature of the
prison system sets up to potentially trigger some of
those
traumatic responses. So pat-down searches,
touching the groin area could very easily trigger a
response that could be interpreted as aggressive behavior,
as a characterological trait, where it might just be a
situational response to an otherwise unusual
type of intervention.
The third group is the alcohol and drug
withdrawal group.
Both alcohol and methamphetamine in a withdrawal phase
or an acute intoxication phase after remand might
present with quite serious health
implications. Methamphetamine particularly,
on reception if you're acutely intoxicated,
you still might be at risk of behavioral disturbance, if
not physiological compromise, potentially life-threatening.
So we're concerned about that group.
Young people as well. You would have read the submission and
the reasons for our concern about that.
But going back to the earlier point, and Ms.
Dyberg's
kept reiterating the rehabilitation need,
we're concerned that the
response of
the DMP decision cuts out a
reasonable rehabilitation opportunity.
And we see corrections as
needing to lead that, and potentially interface with
the-
Mm-hmm
... the health sector, the mental health sector,
to provide an equivalency of care to people in the community.
So as a brief example, just mindful of time, and I want to
leave opportunities for questions.
So-
Thanks.
I have personally been involved in a case that's currently
before the court of a man who committed a
serious index offense. There's no disputing that.
But he had a clear history of autism, autistic spectrum disorder,
and a very significant history of trauma.
So on reception to prison some 20 years
ago, these needs were not identified,
and in the early stages of his treatment, sorry, of his
incarceration, he was subject to strip searches, to
solitary confinement, which absolutely aggravated
his behavior,
which set up a compounding cycle. That the more triggered he was, the more
behaviorally disturbed he became, the more restrictive the practice.
And so the cycle went on to the point of serious self-harm
and suicide attempts in the first few years of his
incarceration.
The nature of,
I guess, potentially any prisoner, but certainly the long-term prisoners,
as corrections priority in terms of psychology interventions
is to address that in relation to risks, needs,
responsivity. So what is the criminogenic
need for these people?
And early in sentencing, this will not be offered,
because resourcing limits this to when they
enter into the parole eligibility stage.
So in this man's case, there were years of lost
opportunity to actually deliver a therapeutic
intervention. As
a result of that,
he became designated as a problematic prisoner,
countertransference,
and spent many years in solitary confinement.
And now that he's past parole eligibility,
people are having to go back and start again and actually deliver
the treatment needs needed for his
trauma and autism, simply to get him
prepared to begin to do the criminogenic
programs which ultimately will lead for his parole.
So it's sort of
back to front, if you like. So we would
like to
put to the committee
consideration of bolstering the act by
introducing the clinical
voice decision-making into the legal
process, and particularly in relation to the proposed
panels. We see it as a shortcoming that there isn't clear and
overt psychiatric or psychological
job descriptions, if that's the right word, expertise as a
requirement for the panel. Whether or not it's independent as much
Dieberg says, I guess
that's not for us. But I think the gap is there is
no psychiatric, psychological expertise in
that.
Okay.
Which is probably the bottom line, and the resource implications, so
it goes, but yeah.
All right. I'm going to
ask Honorable Dr. Duncan Webb to-
I just-
... ask a question.
I wondered, we heard from a parent of a prisoner
who didn't necessarily record any mental health issues prior to
incarceration, but recounted
more than just anxiety, but some real negative mental health
impacts, particularly of the seclusion in the Pere Unit.
Can you just talk to us from your experience and knowledge
of the mental health impacts and whether it can do lasting harm to
have people in that kind of incarceration?
Well, the simple answer is yes, it can, and yes, it
does. Upon reception, there are
triage tools that the
prison administers. They are not
absolutely reliable, and people can slip through that first triage
point.
By the time you're getting to the most restrictive
intervention,
restriction, lockdown, HDU management, that could be
a second point to get a higher level of
assessment rather than triage where
potentially those mental health issues are more clearly identified.
But whether minor, moderate, or severe
on reception, without doubt-
Yeah
... the environment might exacerbate those will be worse if they're locked down,
and that invites the, what are the alternatives?
What are the treatment options that could be provided in lieu of the lockdown?
Back to the role of the specialist on the panel.
Hey, James, I've got to thank you again for making a contribution.
It's always good to have you
presenting to the committee. Thank you very much.
Thank you.
Right.
Members, we've now got a telephone call from Mr.
Hughes, Callum Hughes, who I hope is on the line here.
Right, we're just calling into the unit now.
It's about the end of time.
Kia ora. 13 and 14 speaking with T. How can I help?
We're after Callum
Hughes, please. This is-
Yeah, hold the line.
Thank you
What?
Callum.
Oh, is that you, Callum?
It's me.
Yeah. Hey, nice to hear. You've got the entire select committee
listening to your call. We've got your
submission. So we've got about five minutes with you.
Maybe you might just want to highlight the very significant points that you want to
make to us, then we might have one or two questions for you.
No worries. Thank you for your time.
I wasn't expecting to phone call so quickly.
As you can see by my submission,
the biggest sort of concern I have moving forward is the
definition of a DNP prisoner, and
the category that they're looking to create,
and the restrictions that's going to put on individuals,
I believe
is a very, very dangerous procedure moving forward.
The whole solitary confinement,
limited contact, very little oversight
about
what defines somebody as that,
as well as the length of time once that decision's signed off by
the person in charge, I believe to be the chief executive
or prison director, then that remains in place for two years.
I think the little oversight and
the very little
looking into that, I think it's just a dangerous precedent if this
amendment is passed.
And also given that now that
they're actually admitting that the last couple of years that they've been doing
this, they've been acting outside the legislation and framework that Corrections
has.
But yeah, there's a lot of things that I believe that these new amendments to
the Corrections Act are-
Members
... creating, I believe, to be a little bit unfair and sort of bring
into question
the minimum entitlements for prisoners, and the treatment of prisoners, and the
Nelson Mandela act, and all those sort of things.
But yeah, I think the biggest one is
deeming somebody a risk internally and
externally.
It's a very gray area. Like it's not
coming down to individual's opinions,
not law,
and this
added to the amendments, yep, this amendment's-
Ah
... going to make it very difficult moving forward.
All right. Hey, thank you. We've got a couple of questions.
First one is from Dr Tracey McLennan.
Thank you, and thank you for taking the time.
You mentioned that
prior to this bill,
that Peru was still operating and therefore
didn't have any legislation
guarding that or guiding that.
So what would need to happen
to make you feel more comfortable that Corrections
could make a designation, a
DNP?
Who would need to be on that panel and who do you think should make that
decision
if you think that it's even needed?
That's a very good question.
I feel that
the final say
should be the
panels, not the panel
advising their outcome and then still the final decision coming
down to the chief executive. I still feel
that that final decision needs to be a
broader spectrum, not one person.
I also feel that
the
long-term effects of the individual and what they're
trying to class needs to be more consideration, maybe a psychiatric
people need to be involved, maybe more reports.
And secondly, I believe the
descriptions to justify whether
somebody is an undue risk to public safety or
prison safety is a bit of a broad spectrum.
So you've got two issues that you're raising.
One is the process for who a- actually
makes the ultimate decision, which is what Tracey just asked you.
The other assertion you made b- at the outset
was around the actual classifications themselves.
Correct.
So can you just be a little bit more specific about what your
concern is?
I just feel that it's a very, very
broad spectrum
to be able to class somebody as a DNP given
their profile within a prison or their previous history.
It's not necessarily what they have done or what they are doing.
A lot of it becomes down to what they believe could be a
risk.
Mm.
They have-- Somebody can be put as a DNP without even
committing an offense,
or because they believe that is an undue
risk to public safety or prison safety.
And that really in itself,
does that warrant somebody putting
in
isolat- c- cell confinement and isolation for up to two years
with no contact visits with their families, no contact visits with their kids,
no interaction with other people, all because somebody
may have influence as a gang member or as an individual?
That's where I struggle with, and I don't think
the causes and the things being put into this amendment back
warrant that just yet. Maybe it needs further looking into.
Maybe it needs some independent parties to look at it.
Maybe it needs some consultation with people that are currently in the perd, to how
it's affecting them.
And that's where I think it's been rushed through so quickly without that proper
independent oversight.
All right. Hey, thank you. I've got to just congratulate you.
Very worthy issues that you've raised with the committee this morning.
And thank you for making the time.
And having the first-hand knowledge is very relevant and
pertinent to the committee. So thank you for your time this morning, Callum.
Thank you very much, and I look forward to the outcomes of this committee.
Okay. Thank you.
Thanks.
Right. Now,
I'm going to have to ask you to leave because we're n- about to go now
into private-
We're finished
... into private session.
Closing now.
But thank you for your submission this morning,
Mr Boniek.
I thought you stopped.
I think we're finished
